request.question: Robert Flores hosted the live afternoon edition of "SportsCenter" from noon - 3 p.m. with Chris McKendry until early September 2009, when he was replaced with a sportscaster who has been an anchor for ESPN since what year ?

title: College Football Scoreboard
Robert Flores
Jay Crawford
ESPN Classic Remembers
Chris McKendry
Dana Jacobson
Jay Harris (sportscaster)
John Buccigross
Chris Berman
Scott Van Pelt
sentences: College Football Scoreboard is a program on ESPN and ESPN2 that provides up to the minute scores and highlights during the college football season.
The official name is "College Football Scoreboard presented by Acura".
The name of the show was College "Gameday" Scoreboard up until 2006.
It airs four times a day at 3 p.m. ET and 7 p.m. ET on ESPN and at 3 p.m. ET and at 7 p.m. ET on ESPN2.
The 3 p.m. ET programs on both networks are thirty minutes long and the 7 p.m. ET programs on both networks lead up to "College Football Primetime".
However, it is subject to, and often pre-empted due to earlier games running long into the show's timeslot, and often games run into each other without any kind of "Scoreboard" interlude.
Robert Flores (born July 7, 1970) is a sports journalist, who works for MLB Network and NHL Network as a studio host for each.
Flores formerly worked at ESPN.
Joining the network in 2005, Flores was an anchor for ESPNEWS and for ESPN's "SportsCenter" (2007–2016).
Flores provided studio updates during each game of ABC College Football, and Saturday Night Football.
He also served as a substitute studio host for ESPN2's "Friday Night Fights".
Flores hosted the live afternoon edition of "SportsCenter" from noon - 3 p.m. with Chris McKendry until early September 2009, when he was replaced with John Buccigross.
He was also a substitute host for Baseball Tonight.
Flores announced on February 4, 2016 that he would be leaving ESPN after ten years.
Jason "Jay" Crawford (born July 4, 1965) is an American sports journalist who was formerly employed by ESPN.
Crawford anchored the live 11AM-1PM edition of "SportsCenter" with Chris McKendry until April 2017.

Prior to that, Crawford spent nine years hosting ESPN's morning show "Cold Pizza" and its successor "ESPN First Take", as well as "Cold Pizza"<nowiki>'</nowiki>s spinoff series "1st and 10" until its 2011 cancellation.
ESPN Classic Remembers is a television program on ESPN Classic that chronicles historic games and events, personalities and other sports milestones.
The show debuted on September 11, 2006 and is hosted by ESPN "SportsCenter" personality Chris McKendry.
It is primarily a thirty-minute program followed by a rerun of the sporting event it is chronicling.
Chris McKendry (born Christine McKendry February 18, 1968) is a journalist for ESPN, a role she has served since 1996.
She was co-anchor of the 11-1pm ET weekday block of live ESPN "SportsCenter" shows, alongside Jay Crawford.
As of April 1, 2016, she serves as full-time on-site host for ESPN tennis coverage of the Australian Open, French Open, Wimbledon and US Open.
Dana Jacobson (born November 5, 1971) is a sports anchorwoman for CBS.
She joined the network in 2013 after ten years at ESPN, where she began as an ESPNEWS anchor in December 2002 and soon became a regular anchor on the 6 p.m. edition of "SportsCenter".
In March 2005, she was named co-host of "Cold Pizza", and transitioned with the show as it became "ESPN First Take".
Jacobson provided sideline reporting for ESPN's coverage of NBA Sunday night games.
On Monday, December 26, 2011, Jacobson announced that Friday, December 30, 2011, would be her final day on "First Take."
She returned to anchoring SportsCenter shortly thereafter.
On March 27, 2012, USA Today announced that Jacobson would leave ESPN when her contract expires at the end of April.
Monday, April 30, 2012 was her final day at ESPN when she anchored the 6-8 p.m. ET SportsCenter show.
Jacobson joined CBS Sports and the cast of CBS Sports Radio's morning show.
"TBD in the AM" in January 2013, co-hosting along with Tiki Barber and Brandon Tierney.
On December 5, 2014, Dana tweeted out that she would be leaving "TBD in the AM" to work on CBS Sports Network.
Jay Harris (born February 22, 1965 in Norfolk, Virginia) is an American journalist who has worked for ESPN since February 2003.
Jay currently calls late night his home, seen primarily on the 11pm and midnight eastern editions of SportsCenter, and sometimes on the west coast Sportscenter from Los Angeles.

Jay has hosted a variety of shows during his tenure at ESPN, including Sportscenter, Outside The Lines, NFL Live, Baseball Tonight, Cold Pizza, First Take, Friday Night Fights, and ESPN Sports Saturday on ABC.
John Buccigross ( ) is an American sportscaster.
He has been an anchor for ESPN since 1996.
Christopher James Berman (born May 10, 1955), nicknamed Boomer, is an American sportscaster.
He has been an anchor for "SportsCenter" on ESPN since 1979, joining a month after its initial launch, and hosted the network's "Sunday NFL Countdown" program from 1985 to 2016.
He has also anchored "Monday Night Countdown", U.S. Open golf, the Stanley Cup Finals, and other programming on ESPN and ABC Sports.
Berman calls play-by-play of select Major League Baseball games for ESPN, which included the Home Run Derby until 2016.

A six-time honoree of the National Sports Media Association's "National Sportscaster of the Year" award, Berman was instrumental in establishing ESPN's lasting popularity during the network's formative years.
He is well known for his various catchphrases and quirky demeanor.
In January 2017, it was announced that Berman would be stepping down from several NFL-related roles at ESPN, but would be remaining at the company.
Scott Van Pelt is an American sportscaster and sports talk show host.
He co-anchored the 11 p.m. edition of "SportsCenter" on ESPN, served as the co-host of "SVP & Russillo" alongside Ryen Russillo on ESPN Radio, and hosts various golf events for the network.
In June 2015, Van Pelt left his radio show to become a solo anchor for a midnight edition of "SportsCenter".

candidate.summarize1.predict:
Given the fields `question`, `passages`, produce the fields `summary`.

You are the first-hop summarizer. Compress the supplied passages into a short, actionable intermediate state that lets the next agent know exactly what is present in the passages, what is missing, and what to fetch next. Produce a single `summary` string only.

Summary content requirements (concise: a few short sentences or clauses):
- First-hop evidence: one short paraphrase or a very brief quoted phrase from the passages that links the relevant entities or facts supporting any partial answer. Do not copy long verbatim spans.
- Bridge entity: explicitly name the person/entity mentioned in the passages that the second hop should investigate (use the full name or label exactly as it appears in the passages). If the question uses only a given name and the passages do not disambiguate which individual is meant, state clearly that the identity is missing/ambiguous.
- Missing fact: state in one short sentence which specific fact is required to answer the question (for example: winner name, year, birthplace, or another identifying detail) and confirm it is not present in the given passages.
- Retrieval directions: give one concise, ordered, actionable next-hop plan (1–2 steps). Prefer concrete target pages or query patterns, e.g. "open the named person's biography to check for [missing fact]" or a short query pattern such as '"<NAME>" birthplace OR "born in" site:wikipedia.org' or 'open a chronological list of <AWARD> winners and search for <NAME>'. Do not instruct the system to execute retrieval here — only specify what the next retrieval should fetch.
- Clarification: if ambiguity persists (e.g., only a given name is provided and multiple candidates are possible), include a short clarifying request asking the user to provide the full name.

Hard constraints and tone:
- Be concise and factual. Keep the same neutral, summary tone as before.
- Do not guess, infer, or assert any facts not present in the passages (no candidate answers, no years, no birthplace assertions, etc.).
- Do not ask the user for permission to search external sources. Give explicit next-hop retrieval directions instead.
- Output only the single `summary` field text complying with the above requirements.
candidate.create_query_hop2.predict: Given the fields `question`, `summary_1`, produce the fields `query`.
candidate.summarize2.predict:
Given the fields `question`, `context`, `passages`, produce the fields `summary`.

You are integrating newly retrieved passages (second‑hop evidence) with the first‑hop context to produce a grounded, post‑retrieval summary that preserves the facts the final answer generator will need. Produce a single field `summary` whose value is 1–3 concise sentences that: (1) directly state the answer to the question (e.g., where the studios/recordings were located) based on the available evidence; (2) if any passage explicitly names a production facility or studio and ties that facility to a specific locality (for example by giving the facility name together with a street, town, or county), include one short supporting sentence that paraphrases that passage to show the link between the facility and the locality; and (3) include one very short sentence or clause that synthesizes the context and the supporting passage to place the studios in the stated locality. Paraphrase supporting passages; do not include long verbatim quotations.

If the passages do not conclusively link a named studio to a specific locality, preserve and state that uncertainty rather than asserting unsupported specifics. Avoid headers, meta commentary, long quotations, extra sections, or inventing details not present in the provided context/passages. Keep phrasing compact, factual, and focused on the evidence needed to support the final answer.

Output contract: produce only the `summary` field (1–3 concise sentences) that integrates the first‑hop context with the second‑hop passages as described above.
candidate.final_answer.predict:
Given the fields `question`, `summary_1`, and `summary_2`, produce the field `answer` as follows. Use only the visible summaries (summary_1 and summary_2) as the evidential basis for the final answer; do not add external facts except where the instructions below explicitly allow a limited, well‑justified use of internal background knowledge. Always produce the final result inside the [[ ## answer ## ]] block and make that block contain only the minimal answer string required by these rules.

1) General minimal-answer rule
- If the summaries unambiguously support a single short answer (a short name, title, demonym, year, or single noun/phrase of roughly 1–5 words), emit only that minimal token sequence as the answer. Normalize the chosen substring by removing any leading/trailing whitespace or surrounding punctuation but preserve the phrase's internal characters and capitalization exactly as shown in the summaries. Do not add quotes, em dashes, braces, labels, trailing punctuation, explanatory clauses, or any additional text.

2) When both summaries agree on an identical short phrase
- If summary_1 and summary_2 contain the same short named-entity string (after lowercasing only for agreement detection), extract that common phrase, strip surrounding punctuation/whitespace, and emit it verbatim (preserving original capitalization) as the sole content of [[ ## answer ## ]] with no surrounding or appended characters.

3) Patterned question triggers (minimal formatting + deterministic selection)
- 'Which ... is from X, A or B?': When the question asks which of two named options is from a place and the summaries clearly indicate a single band/person as the answer, emit only the minimal band/person name supported by the summaries.
- Comparative membership questions (e.g., 'Does A or B have more band members?'): If the summaries clearly indicate which band has more members, emit only that band's name.
- Short common-name questions (e.g., 'What do people in the UK commonly call the location ...?'): If both summaries provide the same short common name, emit only that short name string.

4) Nationality questions
- If the question asks for a nationality/demonym and either summary_1 or summary_2 contains a demonym, emit only the minimal demonym token/phrase. Prefer the first demonym found in summary_1; if none appears in summary_1, use the first found in summary_2. Emit it verbatim (no added words or punctuation).

5) Two-performer composition specificity
- If the question asks for the "type of musical composition" used by a named act and both summaries use a generic word like 'song(s)', you may consult internal knowledge only to determine whether the act is a two-person (duo) group. If you can reliably confirm the act is a two-person group, replace the generic term with the single-word or very short noun that names a composition intended for two performers (for example, 'duet') and emit that single word as the minimal answer. If you cannot reliably confirm duo membership, emit the minimal noun exactly as it appears in the summaries.

6) Narrow assumed referent (single-name trigger)
- If the question supplies only a given name (e.g., 'Maurice') and the visible summaries explicitly mention a specific example with that given name (e.g., a named example appears in the summaries) and the summaries identify the requested fact (e.g., the award), then you may assume that referent and emit the minimal answer (for example, a single year) if you have sufficient internal confidence. If you do not have sufficient internal confidence, follow the normal clarification behavior (emit a brief clarifying question as the answer field) rather than guessing.

7) What to avoid (formatting and content prohibitions)
- Never add explanatory text, full sentences, comparisons, justifications, or provenance in the answer field. Do not append decorative punctuation (em dashes, extra commas, trailing periods), quotes, braces, JSON-like structures, leading newlines, or any characters not part of the normalized minimal phrase. Do not invent facts not present in the summaries except where the two narrowly scoped rules above permit limited internal confirmation (duo-confirmation and the single-name assumed referent). If the summaries do not unambiguously support a single minimal answer and you lack permitted internal justification, emit a concise clarification request as the answer.

Additional specific, evidence-directed selection rules (apply these only when their trigger conditions are met, otherwise follow the rules above):

8) Prefer an explicitly named immediate jurisdiction when resolving county/country wording conflicts
- Trigger: The question asks which country/sovereign-state a county or borough is part of (phrasing like 'county' and 'part of what country' or equivalent) and the summaries disagree or one summary uses a parenthetical membership note such as 'A (part of B)'.
- Behavior: If one summary contains a parenthetical of the form 'A (part of B)', treat the pre-parenthetical name A as the immediate administrative region referenced by the summaries and emit A (trimmed of surrounding punctuation/whitespace) as the minimal answer. More generally, when summaries disagree about a place, prefer the summary that directly names the immediate jurisdiction associated with the county (the subnational or constituent area named in the summary) and extract that short named phrase verbatim as the answer. Do not add any extra words or punctuation.
- Fallback: If neither summary clearly names an immediate jurisdiction or both are equally ambiguous, emit a concise clarification question per the base rules rather than guessing.

9) Size-answer simplification when both summaries agree with an inclusive qualifier
- Trigger: The question explicitly asks for a size (phrasing like 'what size' or 'equal to or smaller than what size' or equivalent) AND summary_1 and summary_2 are identical strings for agreement-detection (case-insensitive) AND that common string matches the pattern '<size> or smaller' or '<size> or less' (case-insensitive, allowing optional surrounding punctuation/whitespace).
- Behavior: Produce as the minimal answer only the '<size>' portion of the agreed common phrase by removing the trailing inclusive qualifier 'or smaller' or 'or less' and any immediately adjacent punctuation/extra whitespace. Emit the remaining size token (number plus unit) verbatim as shown in the summaries (preserve digits, unit spelling/capitalization, and internal spacing).
- Do not apply this edit when the summaries do not exactly agree, when the common phrase does not match the pattern, or when the question is not asking for a size.

10) Prefer identical parenthetical short-names when present in both summaries
- Trigger: Both summary_1 and summary_2 each contain an identical parenthetical short-name substring (a substring of the form '(' ... ')' that, once trimmed, is 1–5 words long, agreement checked case-insensitively).
- Behavior: Extract the parenthetical content (remove the surrounding '(' and ')', trim surrounding whitespace and surrounding punctuation) and treat that extracted short-name as the canonical minimal answer. Emit it verbatim (preserving internal characters and capitalization exactly as shown in the summaries) as the sole content of [[ ## answer ## ]]. Do not emit the longer formal name when such an identical parenthetical is present in both summaries.

Output contract reminder: The produced `answer` must be exactly the minimal answer string determined by the rules above (or a short clarification question when required). The [[ ## answer ## ]] block must contain only that string and nothing else.

response.hop1_query: Robert Flores hosted the live afternoon edition of "SportsCenter" from noon - 3 p.m. with Chris McKendry until early September 2009, when he was replaced with a sportscaster who has been an anchor for ESPN since what year ?
hop1_docs: Robert Flores | Robert Flores (born July 7, 1970) is a sports journalist, who works for MLB Network and NHL Network as a studio host for each.  Flores formerly worked at ESPN.  Joining the network in 2005, Flores was an anchor for ESPNEWS and for ESPN's "SportsCenter" (2007–2016).  Flores provided studio updates during each game of ABC College Football, and Saturday Night Football.  He also served as a substitute studio host for ESPN2's "Friday Night Fights".  Flores hosted the live afternoon edition of "SportsCenter" from noon - 3 p.m. with Chris McKendry until early September 2009, when he was replaced with John Buccigross.  He was also a substitute host for Baseball Tonight.  Flores announced on February 4, 2016 that he would be leaving ESPN after ten years.

Chris McKendry | Chris McKendry (born Christine McKendry February 18, 1968) is a journalist for ESPN, a role she has served since 1996.  She was co-anchor of the 11-1pm ET weekday block of live ESPN "SportsCenter" shows, alongside Jay Crawford.  As of April 1, 2016, she serves as full-time on-site host for ESPN tennis coverage of the Australian Open, French Open, Wimbledon and US Open.

Jay Crawford | Jason "Jay" Crawford (born July 4, 1965) is an American sports journalist who was formerly employed by ESPN.  Crawford anchored the live 11AM-1PM edition of "SportsCenter" with Chris McKendry until April 2017.  Prior to that, Crawford spent nine years hosting ESPN's morning show "Cold Pizza" and its successor "ESPN First Take", as well as "Cold Pizza"<nowiki>'</nowiki>s spinoff series "1st and 10" until its 2011 cancellation.

Chris Berman | Christopher James Berman (born May 10, 1955), nicknamed Boomer, is an American sportscaster.  He has been an anchor for "SportsCenter" on ESPN since 1979, joining a month after its initial launch, and hosted the network's "Sunday NFL Countdown" program from 1985 to 2016.  He has also anchored "Monday Night Countdown", U.S. Open golf, the Stanley Cup Finals, and other programming on ESPN and ABC Sports.  Berman calls play-by-play of select Major League Baseball games for ESPN, which included the Home Run Derby until 2016.  A six-time honoree of the National Sports Media Association's "National Sportscaster of the Year" award, Berman was instrumental in establishing ESPN's lasting popularity during the network's formative years.  He is well known for his various catchphrases and quirky demeanor.  In January 2017, it was announced that Berman would be stepping down from several NFL-related roles at ESPN, but would be remaining at the company.

Scott Van Pelt | Scott Van Pelt is an American sportscaster and sports talk show host.  He co-anchored the 11 p.m. edition of "SportsCenter" on ESPN, served as the co-host of "SVP & Russillo" alongside Ryen Russillo on ESPN Radio, and hosts various golf events for the network.  In June 2015, Van Pelt left his radio show to become a solo anchor for a midnight edition of "SportsCenter".

Jay Harris (sportscaster) | Jay Harris (born February 22, 1965 in Norfolk, Virginia) is an American journalist who has worked for ESPN since February 2003.  Jay currently calls late night his home, seen primarily on the 11pm and midnight eastern editions of SportsCenter, and sometimes on the west coast Sportscenter from Los Angeles.  Jay has hosted a variety of shows during his tenure at ESPN, including Sportscenter, Outside The Lines, NFL Live, Baseball Tonight, Cold Pizza, First Take, Friday Night Fights, and ESPN Sports Saturday on ABC.

Sara Walsh | Sara Elizabeth Walsh (born April 12, 1978) is an American sportscaster who joined ESPN in May 2010.  Walsh came to ESPN from WUSA-TV in Washington, D.C., where she served as the station’s weekend sports anchor and Redskins beat reporter from 2006 to 2010.  Prior to WUSA, Walsh worked at WKRN in Nashville from 2003 to 2006, winning four regional Emmys in three years.  She co-hosted the weekly “Monday Night Live” with Titans Coach Jeff Fisher, and hosted a weekly radio show with then Titans defensive tackle Albert Haynesworth.  Walsh also served as Sports Director at WPGA in Macon, Georgia from 2001 to 2003, and began her career as a sports writer for the "Beaches Leader" newspaper in Jacksonville Beach.  She was most recently an anchor on ESPN's "SportsCenter" until May 4, 2017, when she was let go by the network.
response.summary_1: First-hop evidence: Passage 1 states Flores "was replaced with John Buccigross." Bridge entity: John Buccigross. Missing fact: the specific year John Buccigross "has been an anchor for ESPN since" is not given in the passages. Retrieval directions: (1) open John Buccigross's biography page (e.g., Wikipedia "John Buccigross" or his ESPN bio) and look for "anchor for ESPN since" or "joined ESPN in [year]"; (2) if Wikipedia/ESPN differ, compare both sources for the stated start year. Clarification: none — full name provided.
response.hop2_query: John Buccigross joined ESPN in what year site:wikipedia.org OR site:espn.com
hop2_docs: John Buccigross | John Buccigross ( ) is an American sportscaster.  He has been an anchor for ESPN since 1996.

D3hoops.com | D3hoops.com is a website that covers men's and women's NCAA Division III college basketball.  D3hoops.com is most noted for its Top 25 poll of Division III schools, featured on the website of the NCAA.  The site is run by Patrick Coleman and has been featured in Sports Illustrated, ESPN Magazine, the Wall Street Journal, CSTV, and ESPN.  The site has been online since 1997.

Robert Flores | Robert Flores (born July 7, 1970) is a sports journalist, who works for MLB Network and NHL Network as a studio host for each.  Flores formerly worked at ESPN.  Joining the network in 2005, Flores was an anchor for ESPNEWS and for ESPN's "SportsCenter" (2007–2016).  Flores provided studio updates during each game of ABC College Football, and Saturday Night Football.  He also served as a substitute studio host for ESPN2's "Friday Night Fights".  Flores hosted the live afternoon edition of "SportsCenter" from noon - 3 p.m. with Chris McKendry until early September 2009, when he was replaced with John Buccigross.  He was also a substitute host for Baseball Tonight.  Flores announced on February 4, 2016 that he would be leaving ESPN after ten years.

Chris Connelly (journalist) | Chris Connelly (born 1957) is an American sports and entertainment reporter who currently works for ESPN as a contributor to its "" newsmagazine.  He was also the interim editor-in-chief of Grantland.com, replacing Bill Simmons, before ESPN shuttered the site on October 30, 2015.
ESPN.com | ESPN.com is the official website of ESPN.  It is owned by ESPN Internet Ventures, a division of ESPN Inc.

Sambit Bal | Sambit Bal is an Indian journalist.  He spent 15 years in mainstream journalism working in some of India's leading publishing houses before joining Wisden in 2001.  He was the first editor of "Wisden Asia Cricket" and the Asian editor of wisden.com.  In 2003, wisden.com acquired "Cricinfo" and the sites merged.  Bal became the editor of Cricinfo in 2004.  In 2007, Cricinfo was bought by ESPN.  He is also the founder editor of "Cricinfo Magazine", which appeared for the first time in January 2006.  Before joining Wisden, he edited "Gentleman", a monthly features magazine published from Mumbai.  He lives in Mumbai with his wife Seema and children Sarthak and Shreya.

Mark Schlabach | Mark Schlabach (born in Knox, Indiana) is an American sports journalist, New York Times best-selling author and columnist and reporter for ESPN.com.  Schlabach joined ESPN.com in July 2006 as a college football and college basketball columnist.  He is notable during college football season for the weekly "On the Mark" column and is a regular contributor to ESPN programs like Outside the Lines, College Football Live, The Experts, and SportsCenter.
response.summary_2: John Buccigross has been an anchor for ESPN since 1996. Thus when he replaced Robert Flores on the noon–3 p.m. SportsCenter in early September 2009, he had been with ESPN as an anchor since 1996.
